ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry exists for Beijing three-offal stir-fry. FOODON_03302051 (stir-fried food) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for cooked offal/organ meats is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for this Beijing stir-fry combination of kidney, liver, and pork tenderloin.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific traditional Beijing offal stir-fry dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Traditional Beijing Three Offal Stir-Fry is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly equal portions of pork kidney, pork liver, and pork tenderloin served as a shared main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Pork organ meats (kidney, liver, loin) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Beijing three-offal stir-fry. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of three USDA SR Legacy entries: (1) Pork kidney, cooked, braised (FDC ID 167903); (2) Pork liver, cooked, braised (FDC ID 167904); (3) Pork loin, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 167905). Assumed equal-weight blend of the three components (approximately 33% each), with an additional allowance for cooking oil (~5 g per 100 g cooked dish) and soy sauce seasoning contributing to sodium. Values represent the cooked dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for mixed organ meat stir-fry with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Beijing three-offal stir-fry. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on the analogous category of high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes with minimal starch content, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classifications for cooked meat and organ meat dishes. Carbohydrate content is low (~3.8 g per 100 g), primarily from starch thickener and soy sauce. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 11.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.4 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~480 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1440 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce, as a full restaurant serving may contribute approximately 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (~3.8 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~30) result in minimal postprandial glucose impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile; however, the high cholesterol content from organ meats (~285 mg per 100 g) warrants moderation in those with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high cholesterol (~285 mg per 100 g) and elevated sodium from organ meats and soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions, as pork kidney and liver are among the highest dietary sources of cholesterol, and the sodium load from soy sauce further elevates cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Traditional Beijing Three Offal Stir-Fry (老北京爆三样, Lǎo Běijīng Bào Sān Yàng) is a classic Northern Chinese dish originating from Beijing's imperial and hutong culinary tradition. The dish combines three pork organ meats — kidney, liver, and tenderloin — stir-fried together at high heat in a wok using the 'bao' (爆) technique, which involves extremely rapid cooking over intense flame to preserve the distinct texture of each component. The kidney is prized for its crisp, snappy bite; the liver for its soft, yielding texture; and the tenderloin for its smooth, silky mouthfeel. The three components are marinated briefly in cooking wine and starch before being flash-fried with garlic, ginger, spring onion, soy sauce, and sesame oil, then finished with a light starch-thickened sauce. The dish is savoury, umami-rich, and aromatic, with a characteristic wok hei (breath of the wok) fragrance. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish with an exceptionally high micronutrient density: organ meats are among the richest dietary sources of vitamin B12, iron, vitamin A, folate, and zinc. The high cholesterol content (~285 mg per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce make it a dish to be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. The glycemic index is low (~30), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled. It is served as a shared main dish in Beijing-style restaurants and is considered a heritage dish of Old Beijing cuisine.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Beijing three-offal stir-fry dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of three USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked pork kidney, pork liver, and pork loin, with adjustments for cooking oil and soy sauce. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein meat dish category from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: per_100g.vitamin_d_mcg is -1 because Vitamin D is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimation. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from the USDA composite. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore) at SGD 33.80 per portion; price data is preserved from the field survey data seed record but is not used as a nutritional source. The food_name has been standardised to a canonical English form; the original field survey listing name '老北京爆三样 Traditional Three Delicacies Stir-Fry' is preserved in aliases. Halal status is marked false as pork organ meats are the primary ingredients. Kosher status is false as pork is not kosher. The dish contains gluten from soy sauce (wheat-based) and starch thickener.

improvement_actions_taken: Standardised food_name to canonical English form 'Traditional Beijing Three Offal Stir-Fry (Bao San Yang)'.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional local names from input food_name field.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 multilingual requirements.
Constructed nutrient values as USDA SR Legacy weighted composite of pork kidney, pork liver, and pork loin (cooked), with oil and soy sauce adjustment.
Estimated GI at 30 (low) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous category for low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving as (30 × 11.4 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 3.4.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Set ontology match_type values to only permitted values: closest_parent and none.

Revised the documentation for per_100g.vitamin_d_mcg = -1 in the notes field to be more explicit and align with the G3 sentinel values documentation rule, stating 'Vitamin D is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimation'.
